submission.title: Best self improvement podcast?
submission.body: Looking for some new podcasts besides joe Rogan, hubberman, lex fridman

comments.body: The Mel Robbins podcast.
I enjoyed The Game Changer Life. Not listened in a while though
Tim Ferris

Psychedelic Therapy Frontiers - It's probably not the 'Best' self improvement podcast but it can be deeply impactful.  It's more than its name implies.  It explores lots of concepts that correlate with a lot of other therapy concepts.
I really like this one. It’s new and smaller but goes into a lot of detail: [meeting the mystery](https://open.spotify.com/show/0O2fe9HfnsEem34crBC8Jg?si=dIthJbVKQ0mbMf5rDP4dDQ)
The one you feed
Thank you. Will check it out
Ten Percent Happier with Dan Harris.
Mindvalley, Diary of a CEO, Tom Bilyeu's Impact Theory, Modern Wisdom
Tara Brach is a good choice.

Genuinely suggesting "the bald and the beautiful" hosted by Trixie and Katya. Hilarious, feminine podcast with a great balance of philosophical musings of what it means to be an older gay man in the drag industry. 

Katya will talk often about overcoming (or not) drug addictions and dark mental health, and Trixie will talk about the pressure to constantly grind and work hard to the point of physical and mental detriment, then having to force herself to have hobbies outside of her work. Occasional political talk that's grounded in their real life experiences as femme gay men. 

So much respect for these two ladies and how they channel their art. I always cheer up listening to them and enjoy their banter and silliness.
Thewizardliz for podcast, Sarah Blondin from Insight Timer for meditation, Habits of a Goddess for affirmations.

submission.title: Ano po best smelling and affordable na perfumes nyo?

comments.body: LV Afternoon Swim. Hahaha JK! Hmm, anong affordable for you? Ang go-to ko ay CK One. Pero kung mga galing sa tiktok, mabango yung NewYork Army hahaha
Ghost Spectre (Nishane Ani dupe), Elnaris Silk Pajama (Gris Charnel inspired), tsaka Sailor ng Father & Son (UM inspired)
Guess Seductive Perfume!!! Very long lasting. Amoy expensive and classy. I think it is around 1.1k
Paris Corner Rifaaqat
Bambini 🤭
My choice, Apogee and Orage, people can't resist complimenting you when you pass by them
Doesn’t suit my personality. I’m an Afternoon Swim kinda guy hahaha
Sakin yung Bench na Cross Over always na gamit ko noon, ngayon nag Andrea's Secret ako
Samahan mo na ng Lewis and Pearl
Ck one
Life is good when we’re having fu-u-un
Pag affordable pwede ba mist? BBW Luminous ☺️
Bench Hula Girl. College scent ko. Tried buying again recently and it still smells great.
Is ghost spctre for men and long lasting?
M here. I like the musky vanilla ni rifaaqat!!

Yep, it is completely unisex tsaka yung performance is beastmode din but hindi rin masyado matapang, goods ang projection niya. Around a day on my clothes tsaka on my skin, tumagal siya ng 8hrs sakin (in my experience)
UNRVL. Its a perfume brand in lazada/shoppee. Long lasting. Yung pambabae smells like omnia.
Gaano katagal itooo?
Mga 4hrs. Nag sspray ulit ako pag lunch 😅
You can never go wrong with F&S
Mga inspired perfumes
F12 from aficionado ❤️
Fiona, affordable talaga bwhahaaha💗
ano smell nito
is it available every guess store?
Oxygyn amber wood very paco rabanne ang dating.
symlabs dyan blue inspired
SDJ perfumes!
Human Fly. Kakaiba yung scent DNA
Bench po yung kay Jinkee Pacquiao.
Tommy
San nabibili yung elnaris
Sa Miniso yung GreenTea perfume very affordable same scent ng EA na green tea..bet ko kasi yung clean fresh scent lang😊
CK All
ok din to amoy Royal (yung softdrink) hahaha. mas gusto ko yung Pacific Chill though hehe

Sa shopee siya mabibili. Below ang link ng store,

[here](https://ph.shp.ee/9T8yqKJ)
Meron sa mga SM Department Store yung BI-ES less na brand. Less than Php1,000. OK yunh scent nung BI-ES 880 nila.
That, I’m not sure if it’s available in every store but I buy mine online
Ano po maganda, bet ko kasing amoy yung soft lang siya. May ganun bang aficionado hehe. Diko bet yung pang men na parang eros ba yun.
Anong platform?
Amazon’s website (the shipping fee cost a lot tho). Other website sells it too but it’s pricier compared to the last time I bought it. https://perfumestore.ph/product/guess-seductive-edt-for-women/
Bench yung kay francine diaz
Lattafa Asad, D&G Light Blue summer vibes since nasa Pinas tayo kaso price ranges from 4-5k, maybe affordable for some. Both for everyday use
Coco Chanel and VS Bare Vanilla! Hahahahaha daming nalingon pag nasa school ako lalo na pag ginamit ko yung Coco Chanel
Yung Eno Parfum, lahat ng nabili ko mabango. Cute pa ng packaging
Nautica voyage and d&g light blue
Zara perfumes. Seoul

Actually perfumes from Fragrance Lab eros flame, sauvage,  byredo blanche, nectarine and flowerbomb are not bad for daily use or tipong 1-2hour errands. 100ml for 250. Admittedly doesnt last long but spray all you can naman.
Ang mga na try ko so far f1 (ck1), f19 (d&g), f60 (jlo) pero fav ko f12 hugo boss talaga kasi di matapang, di rin masyadong matamis at nagtatagal naman :)

For morning po, Apogee po citrus and lightly floral. Parang angel ka na dumaan kapag naamoy ka nila. 😇

For evening po yung Orage, eto sobra intense, like a force of nature lol. Citrus like orange and lemon. Woody with musk and a pinch of floral.

White magnolia, rose at gardenia s scentsmith. Daily use. 1k lang 100ml

thank you! malakas talaga yung pang gabi? I like musky kasi eh but im a girl, i realize my perfumes lean  towards clean, musky, showery scent ng men

di naman nakaka headache yung apogee?
nde po nkk-headache, basta kpg yan gamit mo, nde nila matiis na kausapin ka just to compliment you, kpg nakatalikod sila lam nila na dumaan k since mblis nila mamemorize ung scent mo 🤣
nice yan hanap ko HAHAHAH ty so much!!
Parfums Astraux Chamomile! Soapy but not annoying. It lasts pretty long din in my experience, lalo sa clothes.
Scenttherapy.ph  They have the best dupes.

submission.title: What was the book that changed your whole view about life?
submission.body: Can anyone recommend me a book that that'll fuck up your mind, doesn't leave your mind for days and change your whole perspective about life

comments.body:
Crucial Conversations: Tools for talking when stakes are high
I started this book to improve my conversation in a professional setting, but this book ended up teaching me a lot about personal life conversations, identifying conflict, and resolving in time and many things.
The Power of Now by Eckhart Tolle
Things Fall Apart- Chinua Achebe
Slaughterhouse-Five
Women who run with wolves
Yep. Definitely.
Ishmael by Daniel Quinn
The Deluge, or Ministry For The Future. Those books are in my head daily
Die with zero
Cloud Cockoo Land by Anthoney Doerr
Beloved by Toni Morrison
Well I was 16-17 years old at the time, and it opened my eyes and mind to everything that’s wrong with political systems around the world 🤓

You're not going to believe this, but 1960 and I was 12 the year Starship Troopers was published. I modeled my entire life around that book. All my beliefs and philosophy  were centered on the premise of Starship Troopers. I honestly believe in personal responsibility and duty of citizens to support AND criticize their society as is required. I am a combat veteran (of course!) and just about exactly lined up with Bernie Sanders and Democratic Socialism
The Communist Manifesto - Karl Marx & Friedrich Engels

Factfulness by Hans Risking

It really helped me to understand that the world isn't that bad. It's just media which doesn't inform you about all the positive things that happen.
North to Paradise by Ousman

I never really considered there were "levers" you could pull to manipulate society. I dumbly assumed it was a more straightforward darwinian competition between the 'golden rule' folk vs the 'might is right' would-be warlords vs 'times of crisis' forcing events. Now I can't unthink everything I hear isn't outcome-based rather than coming from an honest (if malformed) worldview.
Tuesdays with Morrie

- the first ever self help book i read- it’s all in your head, get out of your way by russ

- i’m glad my mom died by jennette mccurdy 

- conversations on love by natasha lunn

- the untethered soul  michael alan singer
I loved 1984… and liked Brave New World even more, personally.

I love Siddharta. That definitely stayed with me for ages! 

1984 + Animal Farm and Brave New World are all good, too!
The bible.
I Am That, by Nisargadatta Maharaj
Throwing in some more George Orwell with Keep the Aspidistras Flying.
Tender is the Flesh. Domestic animals go extinct, so guess what (who) they breed for meat?

When did you first read it? How many years ago was it? I have read it myself for the first time ever about 4 years ago and yes I agree it has some really valid points about society but it wasn’t a transformational book to me. It didn’t shift my thinking as I pretty much thought the same before reading it as after. I’m therefore very surprised to hear so many feel like it changed anything for them. To me it was a book that described the fear I have always had for society if everything went really down hill. I think it becomes more and more relevant as the years pass and online survaliance and technology keeps evolving. Maybe I have always been a cynic, skeptic and cautious about trusting institutions.
That makes sense. It’s an age where a book like that can maaaivky affect how you view the world and shape how you see society in general.
Caste by Isabel Wilkerson and Spark by Dr. John Ratey.
Tender is the Flesh. Domestic animals go extinct, so guess what (who) they breed for meat?
Tuesdays with morrie
Thinking Fast and Slow
Being mortal Atul Gawande

*Thinking, Fast and Slow* by Daniel Kahneman if you want to question the existence of free will

*Being Mortal* by Atul Gawande & *The Road* by Cormac McCarthy for the value & fragility of human life.

*Exhalation* by Ted Chiang to question truth (especially the entry “The Truth of Fact, the Truth of Feeling”)

When I was around 12, I read the Alchemist and thought it was very profound. Catch 22 was a book I still think about a lot. I don't think there's one specific book but with each new book I read, my worldview changes in some way.
Atlas shrugged.
Yeap it was totally like that :)
Homo deus

I first read *1984* when I was a sophomore in college in 1987. So, I was 19 years old. I wouldn't say I was "sheltered" growing up, but it was a much different time back then. A simpler time. And I was not yet a jaded adult. Perhaps if I'd come of age in the post 9/11, internet age, it would've been banal.

1984
A Prayer for Owen Meany
The Goldfinch
The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue
The Book of Joy, by the Dalai Lama, Desmond Tutu, and Douglas Abrams.

How to analyze people on sight  and how to get anything You want - elsie Benedict

Becoming Supernatural - joe dispenza

The kybalion

Slave species of god - Michael Tellinger

Zecharia sitchin's books

Stoicism related books like Marcus Aurelius' Meditations

Into the wild - Jon krakauer

48 laws of power - Robert green
Yes, I agree about *11/22/63* by Stephen King. Since I am not a fan of horror, I am not into most of King's other work. But this book is a modern classic. I took my time and savored reading it.
"Im Westen nichts neues" (All Quiet on the Western Front) - have a little history lookup around it, makes for a perfect read.
Yes, Zinn's *A People's History of the United States* is an eye-opening read. It taught me how sanitized so much of the history taught in public school is.
Some parts of that book are a lot less funny when your chain of command really is like that
Can you tell us more about your life and what more do you belive ?
City of Bones. I was 12 and felt I had a place in the world. It’s very 2008 coded tho.
Why?
Shantaram by Gregory David Roberts was truly a "wow" experience for me.

The Demon Haunted World is a great one. It's enlightening in it's own right, I'I'm in front ve also found.its a good recommendation for well meaning friends/family who've found themselves on the more conspiratorial oa
Johnny got his gun
The subtle art of not giving a fuck

Very different than other suggestions here but: 

Together by Vivek Murthy 

Being Mortal by Atul Gawande 

Both made me entirely rethink what I’m prioritizing and have important conversations with those around me.
Ending Aging by Aubrey de Grey. The Open Library page is [here](https://openlibrary.org/works/OL12284524W/Ending_Aging?edition=key%3A/books/OL17932740M).
Night by elie wiesel
Here's a list of places where communism succeeded and contributed to the welfare of people:
“Fahrenheit 451” by Ray Bradbury. It felt like an alarming potential reality to me. Now, as an English teacher in Florida schools, where censorship is out of control, it feels just as important.
Many of Vonneguts works.
Brave new world
Still a good book though👍🏻
I think speaker for the dead is basically the only book thats had a long lasting impact on me. Mainly about personal relationships and how we treat and view ”others”.
One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovitch by Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn
Adding Lies My Teacher Told Me too
The Next American Revolution by Grace Lee Boggs

A Short Stay In Hell, nice shirt read but it’s such an honest and existential approach to life. Really helped me deal with how pointless and monotonous life can sometimes be and how we create our own meaning and find purpose in just simply trying our best!
#flatbois
The outsiders for sure
Meditations by Marcus Aurelius
The shape of snakes.
Anna Karenina, The Selfish Gene and “The Philosophy of Schopenhauer” by Bryan Magee
Ishmael by Daniel Quinn

Definitely. Old woman AF medic/recruiter (1967-1977), Viet Nam era. We tended to be cynical ... it came with the career field. 

My favorite series is MASH. Proficiency along with angry compassion was very real. Example; we ferried VN wounded from the flight line to the hospital 2-3X a week. Ambulatory went in busses, litter cases in ambulances. 

These guys came from the battlefield to MASH units to the Philippines, patched up and when they were stable flown back to the states. Eglin AFB was an ortho and psych hospital. 

In the midst of that Command decided we needed a mock catastrophe drill. They pulled soldiers from on base to impersonate casualties. We had litters lined up down the hall from the ER on the floor. They called in all surgeons; the MOD was there. The head surgeon was furious. This was late at night Don't remember the time ....

He strode up the hall yelling. "I simulate all these soldiers dead! Get them out to the ambulance bay and call for refrigerated trucks! This drill is over!"

The Republic by Plato

Flowers for Algernon

Brave New World
I read and enjoyed this book, too. How did it change your views on life? Curious as to how others could interpret it.

I was a freshman in high school when I read 1984 and brave new works.  Those books plus my teachers who had us read those books absolutely altered the way I view the world.  My sense f questioning things and critical thinking especially. Amazing time for me.

I've thought about this book daily for the last several years. Whenever a completely random thought pops in my mind, it's always followed by "somewhere in that library, there's an entire book about this one thought waiting to be found... probably many trillions of light years away."
Labyrinths, then, for sure.

>!I thought how crappy it would be to be brainwashed into dying for my country that took so much from me and yet loving it as I perished for its false beliefs.!<It made an impact. Sometimes it feels like the modern United States, because it's riddled with propaganda that doesn't quite believe in its own idealism. We feel passionate for what we have because there is nothing better. And we're protective of what we could lose and not what we could gain, thus preventing change for the better.
The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue
Animal Farm makes me so sad. I’ll always remember the poor horse.
I’m reading Siddhartha now but I can’t get past the beginning. I always see it recommended though n
It's cliche, but I'm surprised no one has said the Meditations by Marcus Aurelius.
When Breath Becomes Air
Sapiens by Yuval Noah Harari
The three most important Indian Spiritual Philosophy. I read and loved them.

Trying to not really give any spoilers:



But essentially none of this matters. Especially after reading the last chapter. It doesn’t make me think that this is all pointless in a dreadful way. I think of that book when something bad/annoying happens. That whatever annoyance I’m dealing with is such a minuscule pin drop when comparing it to a lifetime. Obviously somethings are extremely impactful and can’t be shooed away with that mentality but majority of life’s little obstacles can.

I just read The Bhavaghad Gita 101 by Matthew Barnes and it was a great introduction to the spiritual philosophy of Hinduism. The book was more of a summary than an actual translation to put everything in simple terms. 

I will read an English translation next and am excited to read the Vedas and Upanishads. It has touched my heart and resonated with me much more than christianity ever has.

This was the first spiritual book I read after a long dark existential period and it was like a breath of fresh air for me. When my boyfriend at the time made fun of me for reading it, I knew we were over.

The Story of Your Life by Ted Chiang. Would you change your actions if you knew the consequence?

The movie “Arrival” was based on it. Heartbreaking.
Notes from Underground by Dostoevsky
East of Eden
Conversations with God

If you want a philosophical understanding and really want to get to the crux of how The Bhagavad Gita can be applied to everyday life, read The Paths To God: Living The Bhagavad Gita by Ram Dass

If you want the most accurate Sanskrit-> English get The Bhagavad Gita by Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan.
Almost every nonfiction book. Flatland if you like fiction
Half of the references I see to Tolle are mocking him as a new age empty headed scam artist, while the other half claim his ideas are life changing. Not sure what to think.
The Alchemist, Man’s Search for Meaning, and The Way of Kings. I read those when I was deployed in the Navy and they helped me through some rough days

There are probably others but the one that comes to mind is *Sapiens* by Yuval Noah Harari. It got me thinking about the world in ways I hadn't considered before, such as the idea that progress is neither necessarily good nor inevitable.
I just completed reading Siddhartha today morning. I felt like it calmed my soul while reading the book.
Alchimist by Paulo Cholelo
“The Creature From Jekyll Island”
Spiritual Enlightenment The Damndest Thing by Jed McKenna
Same here, and “How the Secret changed my life”.

Between life and death by Dolores Cannon.

You will never be afraid of death again!!
Number one is decent but the other two are part common sense and part high school philosophy repackaged with cool words added on top. Atomic fucking wow this is so cool.
What about this one?
"Dry" by Neal Shusterman has stuck in my brain for the last 5 years, especially every time I shower, do dishes, and drink water. I live in California, so it hit hard.

The Jungle by Upton Sinclair left some impressions.

Edit: Realized it's tagged as nonfiction, this is fiction based in reality.

A good nonfiction option might be The Rhetoric of Aristotle but I don't think it's what you're looking for.

Am I crazy that I didn’t enjoy (and couldn’t finish) 1984? Besides it being utterly depressing, there was nothing compelling about it. Funnily enough I’m enjoying Severance (the series) that has a distinctly Orwellian vibe.
the siddharta, one of the best books ive ever read. each year i keep coming back to give it a fresh read

That’s makes so much sense. Especially your age and the way society was back then before 9/11. I was much older when i read it so I already had some established and sceptical believes reagarding th world and my own government. Since I was I child i was always sceptical of authorities and wasn’t afraid to question them or rebel against them but reading the book when I was much older didn’t shift anything for me merely confirmed my believes and reminded me how important critical thinking is. It was a book I won’t forget because it does stand out but not life changing at all.

The Three body problem 
Any one of the Dostoevsky books
11/22/63 is such a high point in King’s latter day career. I love everything about that book. His ability to evoke a time and place is better than any other author I’ve read
Ooh, and I do love Carl Sagan and he did change my world view… just haven’t read this one. But Cosmos blew my mind when I read it in my teens!

The central message of capital being useless without labour and workers owning the means of production are still 100% correct and more relevant today than they were 150 years ago. And Marx always intended for communism to be evolve from post-industrial Britain not to be bastardised and forced on agrarian society’s as a mechanism for enforced industrialisation.
And certainly not sustainable!

I did my final English paper on a compare and contrast between Down and Out in Paris and London (highly recommend) and The Jungle. 2 books that are even more relevant now than when I read them 25 years ago!
The Bible
Never cried so much in a book
its truly an amazing book🥹 probably my favorite of his

Man's Search for Meaning by Victor Frankel

The author was a psychologist in the Nazi death camps and his job was talk suicidal prisoners out of wanting to kill themselves. He took this job seriously and developed techniques that he shares in this book to give hope to the hopeless.
That's a fascinating response. Thanks for sharing.
Anything Haruki Murakami

It might be slightly dumb, but it's true. **Six of Crows by Leigh Bardugo**. For those unacquainted with the book, the main character is disabled. I'm read this book looking for disability representation, coming to terms with my own health and mobility going down the drain. I adored the representation and I've never come across representation as good as it before, and I doubt I'll come across it again. It made me feel seen, and like a badass. This author is also disabled so it's no surprise that it's that good. I met Leigh Bardugo at a book signing and I really wanted to tell her this, but I'm a very awkward person and could not spit it out.

What he writes is true and he highlights and simplifies some insightful concepts and truths from taoism that is otherwise harder to grasp.

I think it's more the format he is criticized for - being slightly selfobsessed and capitalistic defeats the point of the taoism he is attempting to promote as his own.
i’ve heard a lot about this book but haven’t had the chance to read it yet. how did this book impact your views on life?
Jakarta Method by Vincent Bevins. Nearly traumatized me.
I feel like this should be follow up with Hitler’s American Model by James Whitman. The history of the US is SO much darker than people realize.
Makes me happy to see this here. Correct answer.

Prophet Song, which won last year’s Booker Prize. Truly haunting warning about how life would likely play out in a democratic country taken over by a totalitarian government.  What stuck with me was the underlying theme of how people convince themselves not to act in the mistaken belief that things will get better, or at least won’t get worse, and fail to see that the consequences of non-action can be far worse than taking a risk.  While that theme plays out in a political setting, the message struck me deeply as applying to all sorts of life situations
Thanks a lot! And I've loved Orwell ever since! XD
The last rhino. A short read that was assigned but it covers I think the scope of humanity in a very beautiful and tragic way. And it was someone's life experiences.

Have you read the 2 sequals?  Story of B,
My Ishmael
Thank you so much for this!  I will get both of these. I have listened to all of the Here and Now podcast and have been wanting to read one of Ram Dass’ books.
"white nights" by Feydor snaped me out of a funk... And let me see things a bit different
Do yourself a favor and read The Alchemist Paulo Coelho. It was very good.

For me, it changed my outlook on things I perceive as “negative”

I have a different outlook on the dark parts of humans, and especially the dark aspects of my identity as woman.
Day of the triffids my john wyndham
Collapse by Jared Diamond
fooled by randomness (Nassim Taleb)

1984

Yeap poor little guy and it’s a very sad book and
Watership Down

Pierre Bayards 'How to talk about books you haven't read'


It's not a 'fuck up your mind' kind of book but it illustrates with such power how we perceive knowledge and how elitist and classist canon literature is being used to exclude people. It fundamentally changed my perception of reading in general. Of academia. Of life.
Highly recommend
It's always a pleasure coming across someone who resonates with Ram Dass as well!! The community is shockingly still niche. Please let me know how you like the books! Which books are your favorite?
The Bible

submission.title: I am down and sad right now. Can you recommend any songs that can cheer me up?
submission.body: I feel like a failure today kaya kailangan ko ng music boost (kahit ano genre or artirts, local or international) na makakalimutan ko ang araw na 'to. Pwede din playlist kung meron kayo. Thank you in advance. 🙂

* Circles — Seventeen ; It's going to be okay, like the hands of the clock, they'll go in circles, back to their place.
* Brave Enough — Lee Hi ; I say I'm brave enough, brave enough to be myself. And there'll be a dawn after, this long and dark night.
* All I Feel and See — Any Name's Okay ; Life's not what I hope it'd be but I'm taking all I feel and see.
* Better Days — Tom Misch ; There'll be better days, better days, better days.
